content:
Turning **categories into numbers** became essential as statistical models moved from hand-built **dummy variables** in **OLS regression** (econometrics, ANOVA) to large **tabular ML** pipelines.

Early practice used **0/1 indicator variables** for factor levels—today's **one-hot encoding**. Analysts distinguished **nominal** factors (no order) from **ordinal** scales (Likert, grades), mirroring the modern split between one-hot and ordered integers.

**CART** decision trees (1980s) required numeric matrices; practitioners one-hot encoded categoricals so each level could appear in threshold splits. **Label encoding** spread with tree libraries that accepted integer-coded categories, sometimes blurring nominal/ordinal discipline.

**Target encoding** (mean encoding, likelihood encoding) gained popularity in **Kaggle** tabular competitions for high-cardinality fields like city and device id. Competition write-ups highlighted **label leakage** when means were computed in-sample—motivating **out-of-fold** target statistics and **smoothing** priors.

Modern **sklearn Pipelines** (`ColumnTransformer`, `OneHotEncoder`, third-party `category_encoders`) embed the same lesson as scaling: **fit on train**, **transform consistently**, and **serialize** with the model for reproducible serving.

timeline.label: Dummy variables in statistics
timeline.description: Regression with factor covariates formalizes one-hot-style indicators for unordered categories.

timeline.label: ML trees and sparse matrices
timeline.description: Decision-tree ensembles drive one-hot expansion for nominal fields in numeric split finders.

timeline.label: Competition target encoding
timeline.description: Mean encoding for cardinality sparks leakage awareness and cross-fitted smoothing practices.
